children.documentation:
Main method to visit `node` and its children.

It tries to find a `visit_*()` method for the given `node.type`, like
`visit_simple_stmt` for Node objects or `visit_INDENT` for Leaf objects.
If no dedicated `visit_*()` method is found, chooses `visit_default()`
instead.

Then yields objects of type `T` from the selected visitor.
children.name: visit

children.documentation: Default `visit_*()` implementation. Recurses to children of `node`.
children.name: visit_default

result.documentation: Basic lib2to3 visitor that yields things of type `T` on `visit()`.
result.name: Visitor

result.documentation:
Return whitespace prefix if needed for the given `leaf`.

`complex_subscript` signals whether the given leaf is part of a subscription
which has non-trivial arguments, like arithmetic expressions or function calls.
result.name: whitespace
result.signature:
def whitespace(leaf: Leaf, *, complex_subscript: bool, mode: Mode) -> str:  # noqa: C901
    """Return whitespace prefix if needed for the given `leaf`.

    `complex_subscript` signals whether the given leaf is part of a subscription
    which has non-trivial arguments, like arithmetic expressions or function calls.
    """
    NO: Final[str] = ""
    SPACE: Final[str] = " "
    DOUBLESPACE: Final[str] = "  "
    t = leaf.type
    p = leaf.parent
    v = leaf.value
    if t in ALWAYS_NO_SPACE

result.documentation: Generate a normalized prefix string.
result.name: make_simple_prefix
result.signature: def make_simple_prefix(nl_count: int, form_feed: bool, empty_line: str = "\n") -> str

result.documentation: Return the first leaf that precedes `node`, if any.
result.name: preceding_leaf

result.documentation:
Return if the `node` and its previous siblings match types against the provided
list of tokens; the provided `node`has its type matched against the last element in
the list.  `None` can be used as the first element to declare that the start of the
list is anchored at the start of its parent's children.
result.name: prev_siblings_are
result.signature: def prev_siblings_are(node: Optional[LN], tokens: List[Optional[NodeType]]) -> bool

result.documentation:
Returns:
    @node.parent.type, if @node is not None and has a parent.
        OR
    None, otherwise.
result.name: parent_type

result.documentation: Return the child of `ancestor` that contains `descendant`.
result.name: child_towards
result.signature: def child_towards(ancestor: Node, descendant: LN) -> Optional[LN]

result.documentation:
Side Effects:
    * If @old_child.parent is set, replace @old_child with @new_child in
    @old_child's underlying Node structure.
        OR
    * Otherwise, this function does nothing.
result.name: replace_child
result.signature: def replace_child(old_child: LN, new_child: LN) -> None

result.documentation:
Return `leaf` or one of its ancestors that is the topmost container of it.

By "container" we mean a node where `leaf` is the very first child.
result.name: container_of

result.documentation: Returns the first leaf of the node tree.
result.name: first_leaf_of

result.documentation: Whether node is an arithmetic or a binary arithmetic expression
result.name: is_arith_like
is_docstring
result.signature: def is_docstring(leaf: Leaf, mode: Mode) -> bool

result.documentation: Return True if `node` holds an empty tuple.
result.name: is_empty_tuple

result.documentation: Return True if `node` holds a tuple with one element, with or without parens.
result.name: is_one_tuple

result.documentation: Return True if `node` holds a tuple that contains a walrus operator.
result.name: is_tuple_containing_walrus

result.documentation: Return True if content between `opening` and `closing` is a one-sequence.
result.name: is_one_sequence_between

result.documentation: Return True iff `node` is of the shape ( test := test )
result.name: is_walrus_assignment

result.documentation: Return True iff `node` is a trailer valid in a simple decorator
result.name: is_simple_decorator_trailer
result.signature: def is_simple_decorator_trailer(node: LN, last: bool = False) -> bool

result.documentation:
Return True iff `node` could be a 'dotted name' decorator

This function takes the node of the 'namedexpr_test' of the new decorator
grammar and test if it would be valid under the old decorator grammar.

The old grammar was: decorator: @ dotted_name [arguments] NEWLINE
The new grammar is : decorator: @ namedexpr_test NEWLINE
result.name: is_simple_decorator_expression

result.documentation: Return True if `node` holds a `yield` or `yield from` expression.
result.name: is_yield

result.documentation:
Return True if `leaf` is a star or double star in a vararg or kwarg.

If `within` includes VARARGS_PARENTS, this applies to function signatures.
If `within` includes UNPACKING_PARENTS, it applies to right hand-side
extended iterable unpacking (PEP 3132) and additional unpacking
generalizations (PEP 448).
result.name: is_vararg
result.signature: def is_vararg(leaf: Leaf, within: Set[NodeType]) -> bool

result.documentation: Return True if `leaf` is a multiline string that actually spans many lines.
result.name: is_multiline_string

result.documentation: Return True if `node` is a suite with a stub body.
result.name: is_stub_suite

result.documentation: Return True if `node` is a simple statement containing an ellipsis.
result.name: is_stub_body

result.documentation:
Given a `LN`, determines whether it's an atom `node` with invisible
parens. Useful in dedupe-ing and normalizing parens.
result.name: is_atom_with_invisible_parens

result.documentation: Return True if the given leaf starts an import statement.
result.name: is_import

result.documentation: Return True if the given leaf starts a with or async with statement.
result.name: is_with_or_async_with_stmt

result.documentation:
Return True if the given leaf starts an async def/for/with statement.

Note that `async def` can be either an `async_stmt` or `async_funcdef`,
the latter is used when it has decorators.
result.name: is_async_stmt_or_funcdef

result.documentation:
Return True if the given leaf is a type comment. This function should only
be used for general type comments (excluding ignore annotations, which should
use `is_type_ignore_comment`). Note that general type comments are no longer
used in modern version of Python, this function may be deprecated in the future.
result.name: is_type_comment

result.documentation: Return True if the given leaf is a type comment with ignore annotation.
result.name: is_type_ignore_comment

result.documentation:
Return True if the given string match with type comment with
ignore annotation.
result.name: is_type_ignore_comment_string

result.documentation:
Wrap `child` in parentheses.

This replaces `child` with an atom holding the parentheses and the old
child.  That requires moving the prefix.

If `visible` is False, the leaves will be valueless (and thus invisible).
result.name: wrap_in_parentheses
result.signature: def wrap_in_parentheses(parent: Node, child: LN, *, visible: bool = True) -> None

result.documentation:
Returns `wrapped` if `node` is of the shape ( wrapped ).

Parenthesis can be optional. Returns None otherwise
result.name: unwrap_singleton_parenthesis

result.documentation:
Make sure parentheses are visible.

They could be invisible as part of some statements (see
:func:`normalize_invisible_parens` and :func:`visit_import_from`).
result.name: ensure_visible

result.documentation: Returns the type of annotation this leaf is part of, if any.
result.name: get_annotation_type

result.documentation: Returns whether this leaf is part of a type annotation.
result.name: is_part_of_annotation

result.documentation: Returns the first leaf of the ancestor node.
result.name: first_leaf

result.documentation: Returns the last leaf of the ancestor node.
result.name: last_leaf

result.documentation: Returns the furthest ancestor that has this leaf node as the last leaf.
result.name: furthest_ancestor_with_last_leaf

result.documentation:
Replace `regex` with `replacement` twice on `original`.

This is used by string normalization to perform replaces on
overlapping matches.
result.name: sub_twice
result.signature: def sub_twice(regex: Pattern[str], replacement: str, original: str) -> str

result.documentation:
Returns:
    True iff @string starts with three quotation characters.
result.name: has_triple_quotes

result.documentation:
Splits string into lines and expands only leading tabs (following the normal
Python rules)
result.name: lines_with_leading_tabs_expanded
fix_docstring
result.signature: def fix_docstring(docstring: str, prefix: str) -> str

result.documentation:
Pre-conditions:
    * assert_is_leaf_string(@string)

Returns:
    @string's prefix (e.g. '', 'r', 'f', or 'rf').
result.name: get_string_prefix

result.documentation:
Checks the pre-condition that @string has the format that you would expect
of `leaf.value` where `leaf` is some Leaf such that `leaf.type ==
token.STRING`. A more precise description of the pre-conditions that are
checked are listed below.

Pre-conditions:
    * @string starts with either ', ", <prefix>', or <prefix>" where
    `set(<prefix>)` is some subset of `set(STRING_PREFIX_CHARS)`.
    * @string ends with a quote character (' or ").

Raises:
    AssertionError(...) if the pre-conditions listed above are not
    satisfied.
result.name: assert_is_leaf_string

result.documentation: Make all string prefixes lowercase.
result.name: normalize_string_prefix

result.documentation:
Prefer double quotes but only if it doesn't cause more escaping.

Adds or removes backslashes as appropriate. Doesn't parse and fix
strings nested in f-strings.
result.name: normalize_string_quotes

result.documentation: Replace hex codes in Unicode escape sequences with lowercase representation.
result.name: normalize_unicode_escape_sequences

result.documentation:
Return the width of a single character as it would be displayed in a
terminal or editor (which respects Unicode East Asian Width).

Full width characters are counted as 2, while half width characters are
counted as 1.  Also control characters are counted as 0.
result.name: char_width

result.documentation:
Return the width of `line_str` as it would be displayed in a terminal
or editor (which respects Unicode East Asian Width).

You could utilize this function to determine, for example, if a string
is too wide to display in a terminal or editor.
result.name: str_width

result.documentation:
Count the number of characters in `line_str` that would fit in a
terminal or editor of `max_width` (which respects Unicode East Asian
Width).
result.name: count_chars_in_width
result.signature: def count_chars_in_width(line_str: str, max_width: int) -> int

result.documentation:
Describes a piece of syntax that is a comment.

It's not a :class:`blib2to3.pytree.Leaf` so that:

* it can be cached (`Leaf` objects should not be reused more than once as
  they store their lineno, column, prefix, and parent information);
* `newlines` and `consumed` fields are kept separate from the `value`. This
  simplifies handling of special marker comments like ``# fmt: off/on``.
result.name: ProtoComment

result.documentation:
Clean the prefix of the `leaf` and generate comments from it, if any.

Comments in lib2to3 are shoved into the whitespace prefix.  This happens
in `pgen2/driver.py:Driver.parse_tokens()`.  This was a brilliant implementation
move because it does away with modifying the grammar to include all the
possible places in which comments can be placed.

The sad consequence for us though is that comments don't "belong" anywhere.
This is why this function generates simple parentless Leaf objects for
comments.  We simply don't know what the correct parent should be.

No matter though, we can live without this.  We really only need to
differentiate between inline and standalone comments.  The latter don't
share the line with any code.

Inline comments are emitted as regular token.COMMENT leaves.  Standalone
are emitted with a fake STANDALONE_COMMENT token identifier.
result.name: generate_comments

result.documentation: Return a list of :class:`ProtoComment` objects parsed from the given `prefix`.
result.name: list_comments
result.signature: def list_comments(prefix: str, *, is_endmarker: bool) -> List[ProtoComment]

result.documentation:
Normalize the prefix that's left over after generating comments.

Note: don't use backslashes for formatting or you'll lose your voting rights.
result.name: normalize_trailing_prefix
result.signature: def normalize_trailing_prefix(leaf: LN, total_consumed: int) -> None

result.documentation:
Return a consistently formatted comment from the given `content` string.

All comments (except for "##", "#!", "#:", '#'") should have a single
space between the hash sign and the content.

If `content` didn't start with a hash sign, one is provided.
result.name: make_comment

result.documentation: Convert content between `# fmt: off`/`# fmt: on` into standalone comments.
result.name: normalize_fmt_off

result.documentation:
Convert content of a single `# fmt: off`/`# fmt: on` into a standalone comment.

Returns True if a pair was converted.
result.name: convert_one_fmt_off_pair

result.documentation:
Starting from the container of `leaf`, generate all leaves until `# fmt: on`.

If comment is skip, returns leaf only.
Stops at the end of the block.
result.name: generate_ignored_nodes

result.documentation: Generate all leaves that should be ignored by the `# fmt: skip` from `leaf`.
result.name: _generate_ignored_nodes_from_fmt_skip

result.documentation:
Determine whether formatting is switched on within a container.
Determined by whether the last `# fmt:` comment is `on` or `off`.
result.name: is_fmt_on

result.documentation: Determine if children have formatting switched on.
result.name: children_contains_fmt_on

result.documentation:
Returns:
    True iff one of the comments in @comment_list is a pragma used by one
    of the more common static analysis tools for python (e.g. mypy, flake8,
    pylint).
result.name: contains_pragma_comment

result.documentation:
Checks if the given comment contains FMT_SKIP alone or paired with other comments.
Matching styles:
  # fmt:skip                           <-- single comment
  # noqa:XXX # fmt:skip # a nice line  <-- multiple comments (Preview)
  # pylint:XXX; fmt:skip               <-- list of comments (; separated, Preview)
result.name: _contains_fmt_skip_comment

result.documentation: Raised when reformatted code is the same as source.
result.name: NothingChanged

children.documentation: Increment the counter for successful reformatting. Write out a message.
children.name: done
children.signature: def done(self, src: Path, changed: Changed) -> None

children.documentation: Increment the counter for failed reformatting. Write out a message.
children.name: failed
children.signature: def failed(self, src: Path, message: str) -> None

children.name: path_ignored
children.signature: def path_ignored(self, path: Path, message: str) -> None

children.name: return_code

children.documentation:
Render a color report of the current state.

Use `click.unstyle` to remove colors.
children.name: __str__

result.documentation: Provides a reformatting counter. Can be rendered with `str(report)`.
result.name: Report

result.documentation:
If our environment has uvloop installed we use it.

This is called only from command-line entry points to avoid
interfering with the parent process if Black is used as a library.
result.name: maybe_install_uvloop

result.documentation: asyncio signal handler that cancels all `tasks` and reports to stderr.
result.name: cancel

result.documentation: Cancel all pending tasks on `loop`, wait for them, and close the loop.
result.name: shutdown

result.documentation: Reformat multiple files using a ProcessPoolExecutor.
result.name: reformat_many
result.signature:
def reformat_many(
    sources: Set[Path],
    fast: bool,
    write_back: WriteBack,
    mode: Mode,
    report: Report,
    workers: Optional[int],
) -> None

result.documentation:
Run formatting of `sources` in parallel using the provided `executor`.

(Use ProcessPoolExecutors for actual parallelism.)

`write_back`, `fast`, and `mode` options are passed to
:func:`format_file_in_place`.
result.name: schedule_formatting
result.signature:
async def schedule_formatting(
    sources: Set[Path],
    fast: bool,
    write_back: WriteBack,
    mode: Mode,
    report: "Report",
    loop: asyncio.AbstractEventLoop,
    executor: "Executor",
) -> None

result.documentation:
Remove trailing semicolon from Jupyter notebook cell.

For example,

    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.plot(x_data, y_data);  # plot data

would become

    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.plot(x_data, y_data)  # plot data

Mirrors the logic in `quiet` from `IPython.core.displayhook`, but uses
``tokenize_rt`` so that round-tripping works fine.
result.name: remove_trailing_semicolon

result.documentation:
Put trailing semicolon back if cell originally had it.

Mirrors the logic in `quiet` from `IPython.core.displayhook`, but uses
``tokenize_rt`` so that round-tripping works fine.
result.name: put_trailing_semicolon_back
result.signature: def put_trailing_semicolon_back(src: str, has_trailing_semicolon: bool) -> str

result.documentation:
Mask IPython magics so content becomes parseable Python code.

For example,

    %matplotlib inline
    'foo'

becomes

    "25716f358c32750e"
    'foo'

The replacements are returned, along with the transformed code.
result.name: mask_cell

result.documentation:
Return randomly generated token to mask IPython magic with.

For example, if 'magic' was `%matplotlib inline`, then a possible
token to mask it with would be `"43fdd17f7e5ddc83"`. The token
will be the same length as the magic, and we make sure that it was
not already present anywhere else in the cell.
result.name: get_token
result.signature: def get_token(src: str, magic: str) -> str

result.documentation:
Replace cell magic with token.

Note that 'src' will already have been processed by IPython's
TransformerManager().transform_cell.

Example,

    get_ipython().run_cell_magic('t', '-n1', 'ls =!ls\n')

becomes

    "a794."
    ls =!ls

The replacement, along with the transformed code, is returned.
result.name: replace_cell_magics

result.documentation:
Replace magics within body of cell.

Note that 'src' will already have been processed by IPython's
TransformerManager().transform_cell.

Example, this

    get_ipython().run_line_magic('matplotlib', 'inline')
    'foo'

becomes

    "5e67db56d490fd39"
    'foo'

The replacement, along with the transformed code, are returned.
result.name: replace_magics

result.documentation:
Remove replacements from cell.

For example

    "9b20"
    foo = bar

becomes

    %%time
    foo = bar
result.name: unmask_cell
result.signature: def unmask_cell(src: str, replacements: List[Replacement]) -> str

result.documentation:
Check if attribute is IPython magic.

Note that the source of the abstract syntax tree
will already have been processed by IPython's
TransformerManager().transform_cell.
result.name: _is_ipython_magic

children.documentation: Find cell magic, extract header and body.
children.name: visit_Expr

result.documentation:
Find cell magics.

Note that the source of the abstract syntax tree
will already have been processed by IPython's
TransformerManager().transform_cell.

For example,

    %%time

    foo()

would have been transformed to

    get_ipython().run_cell_magic('time', '', 'foo()\n')

and we look for instances of the latter.
result.name: CellMagicFinder

children.documentation:
Look for system assign magics.

For example,

    black_version = !black --version
    env = %env var

would have been (respectively) transformed to

    black_version = get_ipython().getoutput('black --version')
    env = get_ipython().run_line_magic('env', 'var')

and we look for instances of any of the latter.
children.name: visit_Assign

children.documentation:
Look for magics in body of cell.

For examples,

    !ls
    !!ls
    ?ls
    ??ls

would (respectively) get transformed to

    get_ipython().system('ls')
    get_ipython().getoutput('ls')
    get_ipython().run_line_magic('pinfo', 'ls')
    get_ipython().run_line_magic('pinfo2', 'ls')

and we look for instances of any of the latter.
children.name: visit_Expr

result.documentation:
Visit cell to look for get_ipython calls.

Note that the source of the abstract syntax tree
will already have been processed by IPython's
TransformerManager().transform_cell.

For example,

    %matplotlib inline

would have been transformed to

    get_ipython().run_line_magic('matplotlib', 'inline')

and we look for instances of the latter (and likewise for other
types of magics).
result.name: MagicFinder

result.documentation:
Return a directory containing .git, .hg, or pyproject.toml.

That directory will be a common parent of all files and directories
passed in `srcs`.

If no directory in the tree contains a marker that would specify it's the
project root, the root of the file system is returned.

Returns a two-tuple with the first element as the project root path and
the second element as a string describing the method by which the
project root was discovered.
result.name: find_project_root

result.documentation: Find the absolute filepath to a pyproject.toml if it exists
result.name: find_pyproject_toml

result.documentation:
Parse a pyproject toml file, pulling out relevant parts for Black.

If parsing fails, will raise a tomllib.TOMLDecodeError.
result.name: parse_pyproject_toml

result.documentation:
Infer Black's target version from the project metadata in pyproject.toml.

Supports the PyPA standard format (PEP 621):
https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/declaring-project-metadata/#requires-python

If the target version cannot be inferred, returns None.
result.name: infer_target_version

result.documentation:
Parse a version string (i.e. ``"3.7"``) to a list of TargetVersion.

If parsing fails, will raise a packaging.version.InvalidVersion error.
If the parsed version cannot be mapped to a valid TargetVersion, returns None.
result.name: parse_req_python_version

result.documentation:
Parse a specifier string (i.e. ``">=3.7,<3.10"``) to a list of TargetVersion.

If parsing fails, will raise a packaging.specifiers.InvalidSpecifier error.
If the parsed specifier cannot be mapped to a valid TargetVersion, returns None.
result.name: parse_req_python_specifier

result.documentation:
Strip minor versions for some specifiers in the specifier set.

For background on version specifiers, see PEP 440:
https://peps.python.org/pep-0440/#version-specifiers
result.name: strip_specifier_set

result.documentation:
Return the path to the top-level user configuration for black.

This looks for ~\.black on Windows and ~/.config/black on Linux and other
Unix systems.

May raise:
- RuntimeError: if the current user has no homedir
- PermissionError: if the current process cannot access the user's homedir
result.name: find_user_pyproject_toml

result.documentation: Return a PathSpec matching gitignore content if present.
result.name: get_gitignore

result.documentation:
Returns whether the path is a symbolic link that points outside the
root directory. Also returns True if we failed to resolve the path.
result.name: resolves_outside_root_or_cannot_stat
best_effort_relative_path
result.signature: def best_effort_relative_path(path: Path, root: Path) -> Path

result.name: _path_is_ignored
path_is_excluded

result.documentation:
Generate all files under `path` whose paths are not excluded by the
`exclude_regex`, `extend_exclude`, or `force_exclude` regexes,
but are included by the `include` regex.

Symbolic links pointing outside of the `root` directory are ignored.

`report` is where output about exclusions goes.
result.name: gen_python_files
result.signature:
def gen_python_files(
    paths: Iterable[Path],
    root: Path,
    include: Pattern[str],
    exclude: Pattern[str],
    extend_exclude: Optional[Pattern[str]],
    force_exclude: Optional[Pattern[str]],
    report: Report,
    gitignore_dict: Optional[Dict[Path, PathSpec]],
    *,
    verbose: bool,
    quiet: bool,
) -> Iterator[Path]

result.documentation:
Wrap stream with colorama's wrap_stream so colors are shown on Windows.

If `colorama` is unavailable, the original stream is returned unmodified.
Otherwise, the `wrap_stream()` function determines whether the stream needs
to be wrapped for a Windows environment and will accordingly either return
an `AnsiToWin32` wrapper or the original stream.
result.name: wrap_stream_for_windows

result.documentation: Raised when an opening bracket is unable to be matched to a closing bracket.
result.name: BracketMatchError

children.documentation:
Mark `leaf` with bracket-related metadata. Keep track of delimiters.

All leaves receive an int `bracket_depth` field that stores how deep
within brackets a given leaf is. 0 means there are no enclosing brackets
that started on this line.

If a leaf is itself a closing bracket and there is a matching opening
bracket earlier, it receives an `opening_bracket` field with which it forms a
pair. This is a one-directional link to avoid reference cycles. Closing
bracket without opening happens on lines continued from previous
breaks, e.g. `) -> "ReturnType":` as part of a funcdef where we place
the return type annotation on its own line of the previous closing RPAR.

If a leaf is a delimiter (a token on which Black can split the line if
needed) and it's on depth 0, its `id()` is stored in the tracker's
`delimiters` field.
children.name: mark

children.documentation:
Return True if there is an open for or lambda expression on the line.

See maybe_increment_for_loop_variable and maybe_increment_lambda_arguments
for details.
children.name: any_open_for_or_lambda

children.documentation: Return True if there is an yet unmatched open bracket on the line.
children.name: any_open_brackets

children.documentation:
Return the highest priority of a delimiter found on the line.

Values are consistent with what `is_split_*_delimiter()` return.
Raises ValueError on no delimiters.
children.name: max_delimiter_priority

children.documentation:
Return the number of delimiters with the given `priority`.

If no `priority` is passed, defaults to max priority on the line.
children.name: delimiter_count_with_priority

children.documentation:
In a for loop, or comprehension, the variables are often unpacks.

To avoid splitting on the comma in this situation, increase the depth of
tokens between `for` and `in`.
children.name: maybe_increment_for_loop_variable

children.documentation: See `maybe_increment_for_loop_variable` above for explanation.
children.name: maybe_decrement_after_for_loop_variable

children.documentation:
In a lambda expression, there might be more than one argument.

To avoid splitting on the comma in this situation, increase the depth of
tokens between `lambda` and `:`.
children.name: maybe_increment_lambda_arguments

children.documentation: See `maybe_increment_lambda_arguments` above for explanation.
children.name: maybe_decrement_after_lambda_arguments

children.documentation: Return the most recent opening square bracket (if any).
children.name: get_open_lsqb

result.documentation: Keeps track of brackets on a line.
result.name: BracketTracker

result.documentation:
Return the priority of the `leaf` delimiter, given a line break after it.

The delimiter priorities returned here are from those delimiters that would
cause a line break after themselves.

Higher numbers are higher priority.
result.name: is_split_after_delimiter

rawResponse.instructions:
You are an assistant that solves benchmark tasks using only the available operations.
Tasks may include question answering, retrieval, extraction, analysis, or code-related tasks.
Use the available functions or tools to gather the information needed to complete the task.
Guidelines:
- Use only information obtained from the available operations.
- If the available information is insufficient, say so.
- Be concise and precise.
- Do not make assumptions or use external knowledge.

tools.description: List visible repository files and metadata for a RepoQA workspace.
tools.name: list_files

tools.name: get_symbol

tools.description: Read one generated visible file from a RepoQA workspace.
tools.name: read_file

You are an assistant that solves benchmark tasks using only the available operations.
Tasks may include question answering, retrieval, extraction, analysis, or code-related tasks.
Use the available functions or tools to gather the information needed to complete the task.
Guidelines:
- Use only information obtained from the available operations.
- If the available information is insufficient, say so.
- Be concise and precise.
- Do not make assumptions or use external knowledge.

You are an assistant that solves benchmark tasks using only the available operations.
Tasks may include question answering, retrieval, extraction, analysis, or code-related tasks.

content.text:
```python
def make_simple_prefix(nl_count: int, form_feed: bool, empty_line: str = "\n") -> str:
    """Generate a normalized prefix string."""
    if form_feed:
        return (empty_line * (nl_count - 1)) + "\f" + empty_line
    return empty_line * nl_count
```

children.documentation:
Main method to visit `node` and its children.

It tries to find a `visit_*()` method for the given `node.type`, like
`visit_simple_stmt` for Node objects or `visit_INDENT` for Leaf objects.
If no dedicated `visit_*()` method is found, chooses `visit_default()`
instead.

Then yields objects of type `T` from the selected visitor.
children.name: visit

children.documentation: Default `visit_*()` implementation. Recurses to children of `node`.
children.name: visit_default

result.documentation: Basic lib2to3 visitor that yields things of type `T` on `visit()`.
result.name: Visitor

result.documentation:
Return whitespace prefix if needed for the given `leaf`.

`complex_subscript` signals whether the given leaf is part of a subscription
which has non-trivial arguments, like arithmetic expressions or function calls.
result.name: whitespace
result.signature:
def whitespace(leaf: Leaf, *, complex_subscript: bool, mode: Mode) -> str:  # noqa: C901
    """Return whitespace prefix if needed for the given `leaf`.

    `complex_subscript` signals whether the given leaf is part of a subscription
    which has non-trivial arguments, like arithmetic expressions or function calls.
    """
    NO: Final[str] = ""
    SPACE: Final[str] = " "
    DOUBLESPACE: Final[str] = "  "
    t = leaf.type
    p = leaf.parent
    v = leaf.value
    if t in ALWAYS_NO_SPACE

result.documentation: Generate a normalized prefix string.
result.name: make_simple_prefix
result.signature: def make_simple_prefix(nl_count: int, form_feed: bool, empty_line: str = "\n") -> str

result.documentation: Return the first leaf that precedes `node`, if any.
result.name: preceding_leaf

result.documentation:
Return if the `node` and its previous siblings match types against the provided
list of tokens; the provided `node`has its type matched against the last element in
the list.  `None` can be used as the first element to declare that the start of the
list is anchored at the start of its parent's children.
result.name: prev_siblings_are
result.signature: def prev_siblings_are(node: Optional[LN], tokens: List[Optional[NodeType]]) -> bool

result.documentation:
Returns:
    @node.parent.type, if @node is not None and has a parent.
        OR
    None, otherwise.
result.name: parent_type

result.documentation: Return the child of `ancestor` that contains `descendant`.
result.name: child_towards
result.signature: def child_towards(ancestor: Node, descendant: LN) -> Optional[LN]

result.documentation:
Side Effects:
    * If @old_child.parent is set, replace @old_child with @new_child in
    @old_child's underlying Node structure.
        OR
    * Otherwise, this function does nothing.
result.name: replace_child
result.signature: def replace_child(old_child: LN, new_child: LN) -> None

result.documentation:
Return `leaf` or one of its ancestors that is the topmost container of it.

By "container" we mean a node where `leaf` is the very first child.
result.name: container_of

result.documentation: Returns the first leaf of the node tree.
result.name: first_leaf_of

result.documentation: Whether node is an arithmetic or a binary arithmetic expression
result.name: is_arith_like
is_docstring
result.signature: def is_docstring(leaf: Leaf, mode: Mode) -> bool

result.documentation: Return True if `node` holds an empty tuple.
result.name: is_empty_tuple

result.documentation: Return True if `node` holds a tuple with one element, with or without parens.
result.name: is_one_tuple

result.documentation: Return True if `node` holds a tuple that contains a walrus operator.
result.name: is_tuple_containing_walrus

result.documentation: Return True if content between `opening` and `closing` is a one-sequence.
result.name: is_one_sequence_between

result.documentation: Return True iff `node` is of the shape ( test := test )
result.name: is_walrus_assignment

result.documentation: Return True iff `node` is a trailer valid in a simple decorator
result.name: is_simple_decorator_trailer
result.signature: def is_simple_decorator_trailer(node: LN, last: bool = False) -> bool

result.documentation:
Return True iff `node` could be a 'dotted name' decorator

This function takes the node of the 'namedexpr_test' of the new decorator
grammar and test if it would be valid under the old decorator grammar.

The old grammar was: decorator: @ dotted_name [arguments] NEWLINE
The new grammar is : decorator: @ namedexpr_test NEWLINE
result.name: is_simple_decorator_expression

result.documentation: Return True if `node` holds a `yield` or `yield from` expression.
result.name: is_yield

result.documentation:
Return True if `leaf` is a star or double star in a vararg or kwarg.

If `within` includes VARARGS_PARENTS, this applies to function signatures.
If `within` includes UNPACKING_PARENTS, it applies to right hand-side
extended iterable unpacking (PEP 3132) and additional unpacking
generalizations (PEP 448).
result.name: is_vararg
result.signature: def is_vararg(leaf: Leaf, within: Set[NodeType]) -> bool

result.documentation: Return True if `leaf` is a multiline string that actually spans many lines.
result.name: is_multiline_string

result.documentation: Return True if `node` is a suite with a stub body.
result.name: is_stub_suite

result.documentation: Return True if `node` is a simple statement containing an ellipsis.
result.name: is_stub_body

result.documentation:
Given a `LN`, determines whether it's an atom `node` with invisible
parens. Useful in dedupe-ing and normalizing parens.
result.name: is_atom_with_invisible_parens

result.documentation: Return True if the given leaf starts an import statement.
result.name: is_import

result.documentation: Return True if the given leaf starts a with or async with statement.
result.name: is_with_or_async_with_stmt

result.documentation:
Return True if the given leaf starts an async def/for/with statement.

Note that `async def` can be either an `async_stmt` or `async_funcdef`,
the latter is used when it has decorators.
result.name: is_async_stmt_or_funcdef

result.documentation:
Return True if the given leaf is a type comment. This function should only
be used for general type comments (excluding ignore annotations, which should
use `is_type_ignore_comment`). Note that general type comments are no longer
used in modern version of Python, this function may be deprecated in the future.
result.name: is_type_comment

result.documentation: Return True if the given leaf is a type comment with ignore annotation.
result.name: is_type_ignore_comment

result.documentation:
Return True if the given string match with type comment with
ignore annotation.
result.name: is_type_ignore_comment_string

result.documentation:
Wrap `child` in parentheses.

This replaces `child` with an atom holding the parentheses and the old
child.  That requires moving the prefix.

If `visible` is False, the leaves will be valueless (and thus invisible).
result.name: wrap_in_parentheses
result.signature: def wrap_in_parentheses(parent: Node, child: LN, *, visible: bool = True) -> None

result.documentation:
Returns `wrapped` if `node` is of the shape ( wrapped ).

Parenthesis can be optional. Returns None otherwise
result.name: unwrap_singleton_parenthesis

result.documentation:
Make sure parentheses are visible.

They could be invisible as part of some statements (see
:func:`normalize_invisible_parens` and :func:`visit_import_from`).
result.name: ensure_visible

result.documentation: Returns the type of annotation this leaf is part of, if any.
result.name: get_annotation_type

result.documentation: Returns whether this leaf is part of a type annotation.
result.name: is_part_of_annotation

result.documentation: Returns the first leaf of the ancestor node.
result.name: first_leaf

result.documentation: Returns the last leaf of the ancestor node.
result.name: last_leaf

result.documentation: Returns the furthest ancestor that has this leaf node as the last leaf.
result.name: furthest_ancestor_with_last_leaf

result.documentation:
Replace `regex` with `replacement` twice on `original`.

This is used by string normalization to perform replaces on
overlapping matches.
result.name: sub_twice
result.signature: def sub_twice(regex: Pattern[str], replacement: str, original: str) -> str

result.documentation:
Returns:
    True iff @string starts with three quotation characters.
result.name: has_triple_quotes

result.documentation:
Splits string into lines and expands only leading tabs (following the normal
Python rules)
result.name: lines_with_leading_tabs_expanded
fix_docstring
result.signature: def fix_docstring(docstring: str, prefix: str) -> str

result.documentation:
Pre-conditions:
    * assert_is_leaf_string(@string)

Returns:
    @string's prefix (e.g. '', 'r', 'f', or 'rf').
result.name: get_string_prefix

result.documentation:
Checks the pre-condition that @string has the format that you would expect
of `leaf.value` where `leaf` is some Leaf such that `leaf.type ==
token.STRING`. A more precise description of the pre-conditions that are
checked are listed below.

Pre-conditions:
    * @string starts with either ', ", <prefix>', or <prefix>" where
    `set(<prefix>)` is some subset of `set(STRING_PREFIX_CHARS)`.
    * @string ends with a quote character (' or ").

Raises:
    AssertionError(...) if the pre-conditions listed above are not
    satisfied.
result.name: assert_is_leaf_string

result.documentation: Make all string prefixes lowercase.
result.name: normalize_string_prefix

result.documentation:
Prefer double quotes but only if it doesn't cause more escaping.

Adds or removes backslashes as appropriate. Doesn't parse and fix
strings nested in f-strings.
result.name: normalize_string_quotes

result.documentation: Replace hex codes in Unicode escape sequences with lowercase representation.
result.name: normalize_unicode_escape_sequences

result.documentation:
Return the width of a single character as it would be displayed in a
terminal or editor (which respects Unicode East Asian Width).

Full width characters are counted as 2, while half width characters are
counted as 1.  Also control characters are counted as 0.
result.name: char_width

result.documentation:
Return the width of `line_str` as it would be displayed in a terminal
or editor (which respects Unicode East Asian Width).

You could utilize this function to determine, for example, if a string
is too wide to display in a terminal or editor.
result.name: str_width

result.documentation:
Count the number of characters in `line_str` that would fit in a
terminal or editor of `max_width` (which respects Unicode East Asian
Width).
result.name: count_chars_in_width
result.signature: def count_chars_in_width(line_str: str, max_width: int) -> int

result.documentation:
Describes a piece of syntax that is a comment.

It's not a :class:`blib2to3.pytree.Leaf` so that:

* it can be cached (`Leaf` objects should not be reused more than once as
  they store their lineno, column, prefix, and parent information);
* `newlines` and `consumed` fields are kept separate from the `value`. This
  simplifies handling of special marker comments like ``# fmt: off/on``.
result.name: ProtoComment

result.documentation:
Clean the prefix of the `leaf` and generate comments from it, if any.

Comments in lib2to3 are shoved into the whitespace prefix.  This happens
in `pgen2/driver.py:Driver.parse_tokens()`.  This was a brilliant implementation
move because it does away with modifying the grammar to include all the
possible places in which comments can be placed.

The sad consequence for us though is that comments don't "belong" anywhere.
This is why this function generates simple parentless Leaf objects for
comments.  We simply don't know what the correct parent should be.

No matter though, we can live without this.  We really only need to
differentiate between inline and standalone comments.  The latter don't
share the line with any code.

Inline comments are emitted as regular token.COMMENT leaves.  Standalone
are emitted with a fake STANDALONE_COMMENT token identifier.
result.name: generate_comments

result.documentation: Return a list of :class:`ProtoComment` objects parsed from the given `prefix`.
result.name: list_comments
result.signature: def list_comments(prefix: str, *, is_endmarker: bool) -> List[ProtoComment]

result.documentation:
Normalize the prefix that's left over after generating comments.

Note: don't use backslashes for formatting or you'll lose your voting rights.
result.name: normalize_trailing_prefix
result.signature: def normalize_trailing_prefix(leaf: LN, total_consumed: int) -> None

result.documentation:
Return a consistently formatted comment from the given `content` string.

All comments (except for "##", "#!", "#:", '#'") should have a single
space between the hash sign and the content.

If `content` didn't start with a hash sign, one is provided.
result.name: make_comment

result.documentation: Convert content between `# fmt: off`/`# fmt: on` into standalone comments.
result.name: normalize_fmt_off

result.documentation:
Convert content of a single `# fmt: off`/`# fmt: on` into a standalone comment.

Returns True if a pair was converted.
result.name: convert_one_fmt_off_pair

result.documentation:
Starting from the container of `leaf`, generate all leaves until `# fmt: on`.

If comment is skip, returns leaf only.
Stops at the end of the block.
result.name: generate_ignored_nodes

result.documentation: Generate all leaves that should be ignored by the `# fmt: skip` from `leaf`.
result.name: _generate_ignored_nodes_from_fmt_skip

result.documentation:
Determine whether formatting is switched on within a container.
Determined by whether the last `# fmt:` comment is `on` or `off`.
result.name: is_fmt_on

result.documentation: Determine if children have formatting switched on.
result.name: children_contains_fmt_on

result.documentation:
Returns:
    True iff one of the comments in @comment_list is a pragma used by one
    of the more common static analysis tools for python (e.g. mypy, flake8,
    pylint).
result.name: contains_pragma_comment

result.documentation:
Checks if the given comment contains FMT_SKIP alone or paired with other comments.
Matching styles:
  # fmt:skip                           <-- single comment
  # noqa:XXX # fmt:skip # a nice line  <-- multiple comments (Preview)
  # pylint:XXX; fmt:skip               <-- list of comments (; separated, Preview)
result.name: _contains_fmt_skip_comment

result.documentation: Raised when reformatted code is the same as source.
result.name: NothingChanged

children.documentation: Increment the counter for successful reformatting. Write out a message.
children.name: done
children.signature: def done(self, src: Path, changed: Changed) -> None

children.documentation: Increment the counter for failed reformatting. Write out a message.
children.name: failed
children.signature: def failed(self, src: Path, message: str) -> None

children.name: path_ignored
children.signature: def path_ignored(self, path: Path, message: str) -> None

children.name: return_code

children.documentation:
Render a color report of the current state.

Use `click.unstyle` to remove colors.
children.name: __str__

result.documentation: Provides a reformatting counter. Can be rendered with `str(report)`.
result.name: Report

result.documentation:
If our environment has uvloop installed we use it.

This is called only from command-line entry points to avoid
interfering with the parent process if Black is used as a library.
result.name: maybe_install_uvloop

result.documentation: asyncio signal handler that cancels all `tasks` and reports to stderr.
result.name: cancel

result.documentation: Cancel all pending tasks on `loop`, wait for them, and close the loop.
result.name: shutdown

result.documentation: Reformat multiple files using a ProcessPoolExecutor.
result.name: reformat_many
result.signature:
def reformat_many(
    sources: Set[Path],
    fast: bool,
    write_back: WriteBack,
    mode: Mode,
    report: Report,
    workers: Optional[int],
) -> None

result.documentation:
Run formatting of `sources` in parallel using the provided `executor`.

(Use ProcessPoolExecutors for actual parallelism.)

`write_back`, `fast`, and `mode` options are passed to
:func:`format_file_in_place`.
result.name: schedule_formatting
result.signature:
async def schedule_formatting(
    sources: Set[Path],
    fast: bool,
    write_back: WriteBack,
    mode: Mode,
    report: "Report",
    loop: asyncio.AbstractEventLoop,
    executor: "Executor",
) -> None

result.documentation:
Remove trailing semicolon from Jupyter notebook cell.

For example,

    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.plot(x_data, y_data);  # plot data

would become

    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.plot(x_data, y_data)  # plot data

Mirrors the logic in `quiet` from `IPython.core.displayhook`, but uses
``tokenize_rt`` so that round-tripping works fine.
result.name: remove_trailing_semicolon

result.documentation:
Put trailing semicolon back if cell originally had it.

Mirrors the logic in `quiet` from `IPython.core.displayhook`, but uses
``tokenize_rt`` so that round-tripping works fine.
result.name: put_trailing_semicolon_back
result.signature: def put_trailing_semicolon_back(src: str, has_trailing_semicolon: bool) -> str

result.documentation:
Mask IPython magics so content becomes parseable Python code.

For example,

    %matplotlib inline
    'foo'

becomes

    "25716f358c32750e"
    'foo'

The replacements are returned, along with the transformed code.
result.name: mask_cell

result.documentation:
Return randomly generated token to mask IPython magic with.

For example, if 'magic' was `%matplotlib inline`, then a possible
token to mask it with would be `"43fdd17f7e5ddc83"`. The token
will be the same length as the magic, and we make sure that it was
not already present anywhere else in the cell.
result.name: get_token
result.signature: def get_token(src: str, magic: str) -> str

result.documentation:
Replace cell magic with token.

Note that 'src' will already have been processed by IPython's
TransformerManager().transform_cell.

Example,

    get_ipython().run_cell_magic('t', '-n1', 'ls =!ls\n')

becomes

    "a794."
    ls =!ls

The replacement, along with the transformed code, is returned.
result.name: replace_cell_magics

result.documentation:
Replace magics within body of cell.

Note that 'src' will already have been processed by IPython's
TransformerManager().transform_cell.

Example, this

    get_ipython().run_line_magic('matplotlib', 'inline')
    'foo'

becomes

    "5e67db56d490fd39"
    'foo'

The replacement, along with the transformed code, are returned.
result.name: replace_magics

result.documentation:
Remove replacements from cell.

For example

    "9b20"
    foo = bar

becomes

    %%time
    foo = bar
result.name: unmask_cell
result.signature: def unmask_cell(src: str, replacements: List[Replacement]) -> str

result.documentation:
Check if attribute is IPython magic.

Note that the source of the abstract syntax tree
will already have been processed by IPython's
TransformerManager().transform_cell.
result.name: _is_ipython_magic

children.documentation: Find cell magic, extract header and body.
children.name: visit_Expr

result.documentation:
Find cell magics.

Note that the source of the abstract syntax tree
will already have been processed by IPython's
TransformerManager().transform_cell.

For example,

    %%time

    foo()

would have been transformed to

    get_ipython().run_cell_magic('time', '', 'foo()\n')

and we look for instances of the latter.
result.name: CellMagicFinder

children.documentation:
Look for system assign magics.

For example,

    black_version = !black --version
    env = %env var

would have been (respectively) transformed to

    black_version = get_ipython().getoutput('black --version')
    env = get_ipython().run_line_magic('env', 'var')

and we look for instances of any of the latter.
children.name: visit_Assign

children.documentation:
Look for magics in body of cell.

For examples,

    !ls
    !!ls
    ?ls
    ??ls

would (respectively) get transformed to

    get_ipython().system('ls')
    get_ipython().getoutput('ls')
    get_ipython().run_line_magic('pinfo', 'ls')
    get_ipython().run_line_magic('pinfo2', 'ls')

and we look for instances of any of the latter.
children.name: visit_Expr

result.documentation:
Visit cell to look for get_ipython calls.

Note that the source of the abstract syntax tree
will already have been processed by IPython's
TransformerManager().transform_cell.

For example,

    %matplotlib inline

would have been transformed to

    get_ipython().run_line_magic('matplotlib', 'inline')

and we look for instances of the latter (and likewise for other
types of magics).
result.name: MagicFinder

result.documentation:
Return a directory containing .git, .hg, or pyproject.toml.

That directory will be a common parent of all files and directories
passed in `srcs`.

If no directory in the tree contains a marker that would specify it's the
project root, the root of the file system is returned.

Returns a two-tuple with the first element as the project root path and
the second element as a string describing the method by which the
project root was discovered.
result.name: find_project_root

result.documentation: Find the absolute filepath to a pyproject.toml if it exists
result.name: find_pyproject_toml

result.documentation:
Parse a pyproject toml file, pulling out relevant parts for Black.

If parsing fails, will raise a tomllib.TOMLDecodeError.
result.name: parse_pyproject_toml

result.documentation:
Infer Black's target version from the project metadata in pyproject.toml.

Supports the PyPA standard format (PEP 621):
https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/declaring-project-metadata/#requires-python

If the target version cannot be inferred, returns None.
result.name: infer_target_version

result.documentation:
Parse a version string (i.e. ``"3.7"``) to a list of TargetVersion.

If parsing fails, will raise a packaging.version.InvalidVersion error.
If the parsed version cannot be mapped to a valid TargetVersion, returns None.
result.name: parse_req_python_version

result.documentation:
Parse a specifier string (i.e. ``">=3.7,<3.10"``) to a list of TargetVersion.

If parsing fails, will raise a packaging.specifiers.InvalidSpecifier error.
If the parsed specifier cannot be mapped to a valid TargetVersion, returns None.
result.name: parse_req_python_specifier

result.documentation:
Strip minor versions for some specifiers in the specifier set.

For background on version specifiers, see PEP 440:
https://peps.python.org/pep-0440/#version-specifiers
result.name: strip_specifier_set

result.documentation:
Return the path to the top-level user configuration for black.

This looks for ~\.black on Windows and ~/.config/black on Linux and other
Unix systems.

May raise:
- RuntimeError: if the current user has no homedir
- PermissionError: if the current process cannot access the user's homedir
result.name: find_user_pyproject_toml

result.documentation: Return a PathSpec matching gitignore content if present.
result.name: get_gitignore

result.documentation:
Returns whether the path is a symbolic link that points outside the
root directory. Also returns True if we failed to resolve the path.
result.name: resolves_outside_root_or_cannot_stat
best_effort_relative_path
result.signature: def best_effort_relative_path(path: Path, root: Path) -> Path

result.name: _path_is_ignored
path_is_excluded

result.documentation:
Generate all files under `path` whose paths are not excluded by the
`exclude_regex`, `extend_exclude`, or `force_exclude` regexes,
but are included by the `include` regex.

Symbolic links pointing outside of the `root` directory are ignored.

`report` is where output about exclusions goes.
result.name: gen_python_files
result.signature:
def gen_python_files(
    paths: Iterable[Path],
    root: Path,
    include: Pattern[str],
    exclude: Pattern[str],
    extend_exclude: Optional[Pattern[str]],
    force_exclude: Optional[Pattern[str]],
    report: Report,
    gitignore_dict: Optional[Dict[Path, PathSpec]],
    *,
    verbose: bool,
    quiet: bool,
) -> Iterator[Path]

result.documentation:
Wrap stream with colorama's wrap_stream so colors are shown on Windows.

If `colorama` is unavailable, the original stream is returned unmodified.
Otherwise, the `wrap_stream()` function determines whether the stream needs
to be wrapped for a Windows environment and will accordingly either return
an `AnsiToWin32` wrapper or the original stream.
result.name: wrap_stream_for_windows

result.documentation: Raised when an opening bracket is unable to be matched to a closing bracket.
result.name: BracketMatchError

children.documentation:
Mark `leaf` with bracket-related metadata. Keep track of delimiters.

All leaves receive an int `bracket_depth` field that stores how deep
within brackets a given leaf is. 0 means there are no enclosing brackets
that started on this line.

If a leaf is itself a closing bracket and there is a matching opening
bracket earlier, it receives an `opening_bracket` field with which it forms a
pair. This is a one-directional link to avoid reference cycles. Closing
bracket without opening happens on lines continued from previous
breaks, e.g. `) -> "ReturnType":` as part of a funcdef where we place
the return type annotation on its own line of the previous closing RPAR.

If a leaf is a delimiter (a token on which Black can split the line if
needed) and it's on depth 0, its `id()` is stored in the tracker's
`delimiters` field.
children.name: mark

children.documentation:
Return True if there is an open for or lambda expression on the line.

See maybe_increment_for_loop_variable and maybe_increment_lambda_arguments
for details.
children.name: any_open_for_or_lambda

children.documentation: Return True if there is an yet unmatched open bracket on the line.
children.name: any_open_brackets

children.documentation:
Return the highest priority of a delimiter found on the line.

Values are consistent with what `is_split_*_delimiter()` return.
Raises ValueError on no delimiters.
children.name: max_delimiter_priority

children.documentation:
Return the number of delimiters with the given `priority`.

If no `priority` is passed, defaults to max priority on the line.
children.name: delimiter_count_with_priority

children.documentation:
In a for loop, or comprehension, the variables are often unpacks.

To avoid splitting on the comma in this situation, increase the depth of
tokens between `for` and `in`.
children.name: maybe_increment_for_loop_variable

children.documentation: See `maybe_increment_for_loop_variable` above for explanation.
children.name: maybe_decrement_after_for_loop_variable

children.documentation:
In a lambda expression, there might be more than one argument.

To avoid splitting on the comma in this situation, increase the depth of
tokens between `lambda` and `:`.
children.name: maybe_increment_lambda_arguments

children.documentation: See `maybe_increment_lambda_arguments` above for explanation.
children.name: maybe_decrement_after_lambda_arguments

children.documentation: Return the most recent opening square bracket (if any).
children.name: get_open_lsqb

result.documentation: Keeps track of brackets on a line.
result.name: BracketTracker

result.documentation:
Return the priority of the `leaf` delimiter, given a line break after it.

The delimiter priorities returned here are from those delimiters that would
cause a line break after themselves.

Higher numbers are higher priority.
result.name: is_split_after_delimiter
